skill.name: local-dev-server
skill.description: Manage local development servers for Empathy Ledger with PM2 commands, port cleanup steps, logging, and ACT ecosystem workflow guidance.
skill.summary: Run, restart, monitor, and troubleshoot Empathy Ledger local servers with PM2.
skill.author: Acurioustractor

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command patterns because SKILL.md contains Markdown command examples, not executable Ruby or embedded scripts. The command guidance is still security relevant because it directs an agent to run PM2, local deployment scripts, forced port-kill commands, shell alias changes, log reads, and localhost probes. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network destination was found in the reviewed file.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed Local Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill is a runbook that asks the assistant to execute PM2, npm, bash, deployment script, sleep, and log commands. This is legitimate for local development server management, but it can start, stop, and alter developer processes if invoked without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically central to the skill. They appear operational rather than malicious, so the concern is capability risk, not confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Forced Process Termination Guidance
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting steps use lsof piped into xargs kill -9 to terminate any process bound to port 3030. This can stop an unrelated local service if the port is misidentified or reused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact kill pipeline is present in multiple examples. The risk is well understood because forced process termination is destructive and not scoped to a named application.

medium_findings.title: Hard-Coded User Paths and Shell Configuration Access
medium_findings.description: The skill references absolute paths under a specific user home directory, shell startup files, npm installed through nvm, and project log files. These references are not exfiltration, but they can cause incorrect file access or environment mutation on other machines.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The absolute paths and hidden shell file reference are directly visible. The impact depends on whether the user runs the guidance unchanged on a different workstation.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Probe
low_findings.description: The skill uses curl only to check http://localhost:3030. Static network findings are true as local network activity, but there is no evidence of remote calls or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL findings point to localhost health checks. No external host, credential value, or upload behavior appears in the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the cited lines contain descriptive text and a note about port consistency. No hash, cipher, password storage, or cryptographic operation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not perform cryptography. This is a strong false positive based on textual matching rather than executable behavior.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Match Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported system reconnaissance at the cited line, but the line describes rapid code changes and instant feedback. No host enumeration or sensitive system discovery appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is ordinary development workflow language. I did not find evidence of reconnaissance intent at this location.

dangerous_patterns.title: xargs kill -9 Port Cleanup
dangerous_patterns.description: The command pattern lsof -ti :3030 piped to xargs kill -9 forcefully kills any process using the port. This should require user confirmation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same destructive command pattern appears three times. Its effect is direct process termination.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Profile Reloading
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends sourcing user shell startup files after adding aliases. This can execute any commands already present in those files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell profile reference is explicit. The risk depends on user-local file contents, which are outside this skill.

content.user_title: Manage Local Dev Servers with PM2
content.value_statement: Local development servers often fail because ports stay occupied, logs are scattered, and restarts are inconsistent. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured PM2 workflow for starting, restarting, monitoring, and troubleshooting Empathy Ledger services.
actual_capabilities: Provides PM2 commands for starting, restarting, stopping, listing, and viewing logs for Empathy Ledger.
Explains when to use PM2 instead of direct npm run dev during local development.
Documents ACT ecosystem commands that start and manage multiple local projects together.
Gives troubleshooting steps for occupied ports, repeated crashes, stale code changes, and missing logs.
Shows workflow patterns for Supabase, database migrations, test scripts, and local API checks.
limitations: Commands are tailored to Empathy Ledger and ACT ecosystem paths from one workstation.
It does not install or validate PM2, Node.js, npm, Supabase, or project dependencies automatically.
Forced port cleanup commands can stop unrelated processes if used without checking first.
It does not replace production deployment, monitoring, or incident response procedures.

use_cases.title: Restart a Local Next.js Server
use_cases.description: Use PM2 to restart Empathy Ledger after code, schema, or dependency changes and inspect logs for startup errors.

use_cases.title: Recover From Port Conflicts
use_cases.description: Identify when port 3030 or 3001 is already occupied, clear the conflict when appropriate, and relaunch the server.

use_cases.title: Run the ACT Local Ecosystem
use_cases.description: Start, stop, monitor, and review logs for multiple ACT projects through a shared PM2-based workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Start the Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Start the Empathy Ledger local development server using the safest PM2 workflow. Check whether it is already running before starting it.

prompt_templates.title: Restart After Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Restart the Empathy Ledger PM2 process, wait for startup, then check recent logs for errors before I test the page.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Port Conflict
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose why port 3030 is busy. Show me the process first, ask before killing it, then restart the local server if it is safe.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Local Ecosystem Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the ACT ecosystem PM2 status, identify unhealthy services, restart only the affected processes, and summarize the relevant logs.

output_examples.input: Restart the server after my schema fix.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the PM2 process, restarts the Empathy Ledger service, waits briefly, verifies the local page responds, and reports recent startup errors if any appear.
The assistant identifies the process using the port, explains what it is, asks before terminating it, and then starts the expected PM2 process.

output_examples.input: Show me why the local API test failed.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the server is running, runs the relevant local test workflow, reviews PM2 logs, and summarizes the failure source in plain language.

best_practices: Check PM2 status and logs before starting or killing local processes.
Ask for confirmation before running forced process termination commands.
Adapt hard-coded paths and process names to the current user workspace before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run kill commands blindly when a port belongs to another application.
Do not assume the ACT ecosystem paths exist on every developer machine.
Do not use this local workflow as evidence that production services are healthy.

faq.question: What does this skill manage?
faq.answer: It manages local Empathy Ledger development server workflows with PM2, logs, port checks, and ACT ecosystem commands.

faq.question: Does it run production deployments?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on local development server management and local testing workflows.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally guides shell command execution and process termination. These actions are useful but need user confirmation.

faq.question: Does it contact external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The documented network probe targets localhost for a local health check.

faq.question: Can it work outside the original workstation?
faq.answer: Yes, but users must update the hard-coded user paths, process names, ports, and project directories first.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
